title: ON THE WINGS OF IMAGINATION: CONFLICTING SCRIPT-BASED MULTI-ROLE FRAMEWORK FOR HUMOR CAPTION GENERATION
research_questions: How can the General Theory of Verbal Humor, especially script opposition, be operationalized in a multi-role LLM framework for multimodal funny caption generation?
Does hierarchical imagination augmented with humor retrieval generate funnier and more diverse captions than state-of-the-art humor-generation methods and generic LLM reasoning strategies?
Which HOMER modules and humor-relevance scoring components are necessary for performance, and how robust are the retrieval hyperparameters and decoding temperature?
How reliable are LLM-based and human evaluations of generated humor, and does HOMER generalize to meme images while producing low-toxicity captions?

datasets_used.name: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)
Ranked caption pairs
datasets_used.size: split 8:1:1 into training, validation, and test sets

methods_proposed.name: HOMER
methods_proposed.summary: A GTVH-driven multi-role LLM framework for humorous image captioning. It coordinates a conflicting-script extractor, a retrieval-augmented hierarchical imaginator, and a caption generator conditioned on situation, script opposition, target, narrative strategy, and language.

methods_proposed.name: HOMER-pruning / humor-relevance score H
methods_proposed.summary: A pruning mechanism for expanding imagination trees with retrieved joke tokens. The score combines relevance-opposition based on WordNet semantic similarity and conceptual opposition, humor-frequency, and POS-diversity.

humor_theories.detail: HOMER is anchored in GTVH knowledge resources: script opposition, situation, target, narrative strategy, and language; these shape extraction, imagination, and generation prompts.
The conflicting-script extractor explicitly identifies opposing scripts, and the relevance-opposition score rewards semantically related but conceptually opposing associations.
The paper states that script opposition leverages surprise, incongruity, and cognitive resolution, motivating the humor mechanism.

evaluation_methods.detail: GPT-5 is adopted as the primary humor evaluator with temperature 0; GPT-4.1 is also used in additional evaluation. Evaluator reliability is measured by ranking accuracy on human-written caption pairs randomly selected across 200 contests.
Unbiased pass@k is reported for K={1,3,5}; each pass@K calculation is the average value of five trials, with n_i=5 in Appendix B.3.
20 raters scored captions on a five-point funniness scale from 1 not funny to 5 very funny; the main text reports 5600 rating scores and Cohen's kappa κ = 0.49.
Diversity is evaluated using n-gram diversity and NLI Diversity, where non-entailing caption pairs are judged by a RoBERTa-large natural language inference model.
Harmful content is measured with Detoxify across toxicity, severe toxicity, obscene, identity attack, insult, threat, and sexual explicit dimensions.
Statistical significance is assessed with a two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank test on Pass@k scores, and agreement between LLM evaluator scores and human ratings is measured with Pearson correlation.

headline_results.finding: With GPT-4o, HOMER achieved average improvements over seven baselines of 8.62% on pass@1, 6.48% on pass@3, and 5.91% on pass@5.
headline_results.dataset: Humor in AI and Electronic sheep

headline_results.finding: On Humor in AI with GPT-4o, HOMER obtained the best scores across all three ranking groups.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)

headline_results.finding: On Electronic sheep with GPT-4o, HOMER obtained the best scores for both High-Humor and Low-Humor groups.
Human raters gave HOMER the highest mean funniness scores on both evaluated New Yorker datasets.
headline_results.metric: mean human rating ± std on 1-5 scale
headline_results.dataset: Humor in AI and Electronic sheep

headline_results.finding: On the meme dataset, HOMER outperformed all listed competitors.
GPT-5 was the strongest humor evaluator among the tested evaluators.
headline_results.value: 73.5 on Humor in AI; 70.0 on Electronic sheep
headline_results.dataset: Humor in AI and Electronic sheep

key_findings: HOMER consistently outperformed CoT, few-shot, self-consistency, HumorousAI, LoL, Phunny, and CLoT on the two New Yorker benchmarks across four base LLMs.
The full module combination I+D+C+T_im was best in the GPT-4o ablation table, and removing situation descriptions, conflict scripts, or imagination trees degraded performance.
The paper identifies conflict scripts as especially important: the largest performance drop is reported for I+D+T_im, which omits conflict script C.

The humor-relevance score components all matter: removing relevance, frequency, or diversity causes performance drops, and 100% of the joke database gives the best reported pass@k values in Table 22 for all three Humor in AI groups.
HOMER produced the highest diversity scores reported: 3-gram 0.98 and NLI Diversity 91.5% on Humor in AI, and 3-gram 0.98 and NLI Diversity 92.2% on Electronic Sheep.
Detoxify results were low, with the main text reporting total toxicity sums of 0.023 on Humor in AI and 0.015 on Electronic Sheep.
limitations: HOMER struggles with purely formal or inherently non-humorous images, especially when script opposition is difficult to detect.
The paper notes that humor is inherently subjective because individuals differ in their interpretations of humor and images; inter-rater agreement is κ = 0.49.
The hyperparameter analysis states that retrieving too few humor instances gives an insufficient imagination space, while retrieving too many introduces noise such as unrelated entities.
The full model requires three API calls in the imaginator stage and seven LLM calls per output, whereas the naive model can use pre-processed imagination results with zero imaginator API calls.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper evaluates harmfulness with Detoxify over seven dimensions and reports very low toxicity sums: 0.023 on Humor in AI and 0.015 on Electronic Sheep. It also describes a curation policy for the joke database: use publicly available datasets, exclude multimodal or multimodal-application datasets, restrict the corpus to text-only short jokes, remove near-duplicates, and report zero exact-match and normalized overlap with the test set.

artifacts.other: No HOMER code or released dataset URL is stated. Footnotes provide source URLs for ShortJokes, CrowdTruth, and Dad Jokes.

human_samples.participant_count: 20 raters in the main text and Table 13 header; Appendix B.8 later contains a conflicting statement that “12 human raters” scored each method
human_samples.observations: Main text reports 5-point funniness ratings for seven methods and “a total of 5600 rating scores.” Appendix B.8 procedure also states 20 cartoon images × 20 raters × 7 methods = 2,800 data points, creating an unresolved inconsistency.
human_samples.detail: Raters first judged whether each caption was relevant to the cartoon; irrelevant captions automatically received 1.0. Relevant captions were rated from 1 not funny to 5 very funny. Inter-rater agreement is reported as Cohen’s kappa κ = 0.49.

human_samples.population: Human authors/raters represented in public New Yorker Caption Contest-derived benchmarks
human_samples.observations: Humor in AI: 365 cartoons, average 6,044 captions, three ranking groups (#Top10, #200-209, #1000-1009). Electronic Sheep: 679 cartoons, average 6 captions, split into High-Humor and Low-Humor groups from ranked pairs.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses human-written captions and existing ranking information as ground-truth comparison material, but does not report participant counts or demographics for the original caption writers or rankers.

human_samples.observations: Public ImgFlip meme data are used for generalization; the paper reports pass@k against ground-truth meme captions but does not report dataset size in the main text.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes the dataset as containing realistic, comic, cartoon, and synthetic images.

human_samples.population: Creators/contributors of public text-only humor corpora
human_samples.observations: The humor retrieval database contains 335,570 jokes after filtering, cleaning, deduplication, and near-duplicate removal.
human_samples.detail: Sources listed include Pun of a Day, Short Jokes, Reddit Jokes, rJoke, SemEval 2021 Task 7, TED Laughter, HumorNorm, CleanComedy, ShortJokes, CrowdTruth, and Dad Jokes.

judges.name: GPT-5
judges.detail: Adopted as the primary humor evaluation model after evaluator-reliability testing; used with temperature 0. It determines headline pass@k comparisons by judging whether generated captions are funnier than human-written captions. It is also used as an expert evaluator for visual understanding, humor understanding, and stylistic expression rankings in Appendix B.6.

judges.name: GPT-4.1
judges.detail: Used in evaluator-reliability testing and additional pass@k/statistical-significance experiments; reported as the second-strongest evaluator. Temperature 0 is stated for humor evaluators generally.

judges.name: LLaMa 3-8B; Humor-tuned LLaMa 3-8B; Qwen-Turbo
judges.detail: Used in Section 3.1 to compare evaluator ranking accuracy on human-written caption pairs randomly selected across 200 contests. The open-source LLaMa evaluators use token logits/rewards; Humor-tuned LLaMa3 is trained with SFT followed by DPO and assigns reward scores at inference.
Human raters scored generated captions on a 1–5 funniness scale in the human evaluation. Recruitment, expertise, blinding, and ordering/randomization are not reported.

judges.name: Existing benchmark rankings/preferences
judges.detail: Existing human-written caption rankings define the Humor in AI ranking groups and Electronic Sheep High-Humor/Low-Humor groups used as comparison material. The paper does not report the original annotator counts or procedures.

judges.name: Unbiased pass@k
judges.detail: Headline metric computed for K={1,3,5}; n_i=5 generated captions per image; each pass@K calculation is the average of five trials. The metric depends on GPT-5/GPT-4.1 judgments for whether generated captions beat human captions.

judges.name: Distinct n-gram diversity
judges.detail: Used to evaluate lexical diversity of generated captions, including 1-gram, 2-gram, and 3-gram coverage in Appendix B.9.

judges.name: RoBERTa-large natural language inference model
judges.detail: Used for NLI Diversity: the percentage of caption pairs classified as non-entailing.

judges.name: Detoxify
judges.detail: Used to evaluate harmful content across toxicity, severe toxicity, obscene, identity attack, insult, threat, and sexual explicit dimensions.

judges.name: Two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank test; Pearson correlation
judges.detail: Wilcoxon tests are used for significance on Pass@k scores; Pearson correlation is used to compare LLM evaluator scores with human ratings.

generation_selection.n: One selected conflict script/target/path per generated caption; the paper also states n_i=5 generated captions per image for pass@k
generation_selection.detail: The caption generator randomly selects key conflicting scripts and a relevant humor target, enumerates paths in the selected imagination tree, then samples one imagination path before generating a caption.

generation_selection.scope: Main pass@k evaluation on New Yorker and meme datasets
generation_selection.n: n_i=5 candidate captions per image; pass@k reported for k={1,3,5}; each pass@K calculation averaged over five trials
generation_selection.detail: The headline metric estimates the probability that at least one of k randomly selected captions from the n_i=5 generated candidates beats the human caption. This is not reported as best-of-N selection of a single output for the headline pass@k tables.
Human raters scored seven captions corresponding to seven methods, but the paper does not state how the particular caption shown for each method/image was selected from possible generated candidates.

generation_selection.scope: Appendix B.12 fair comparison under same LLM calls
generation_selection.n: HumorousAI: 3 repeats; LoL: 2 repeats; Phunny: 3 repeats; CLoT: 1 repeat; HOMER: 1 output with 7 calls
generation_selection.detail: The paper states that each baseline generates multiple independent humorous captions and selects the best one for each output, with attempts set to match or exceed HOMER’s seven LLM calls. It does not report how the 'best' caption is selected.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used as one of four base LLMs for caption generation. No API snapshot, provider version, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude-4
model_provenance.detail: Used as one of four base LLMs for caption generation. No API snapshot, provider version, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen-VL
model_provenance.detail: Used as one of four base LLMs for caption generation. The paper reports the 7B size but no exact checkpoint, access mode, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: LLaVA-1.5
model_provenance.detail: Used as one of four base LLMs for caption generation. The paper reports LLaVA-1.5 (7B) but no exact checkpoint or inference date.

model_provenance.name: GPT-5
model_provenance.detail: Used as the primary LLM humor evaluator and in dimension rankings. No snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4.1
model_provenance.detail: Used as a secondary/validation evaluator. No snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen-Turbo
model_provenance.detail: Used in evaluator-reliability testing. No snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: LLaMa 3
model_provenance.detail: Used as an open-source evaluator with token logits/rewards. The paper does not specify the exact checkpoint or whether it is an instruct/chat variant.

model_provenance.name: Humor-tuned LLaMa 3
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned by the authors using SFT followed by DPO on ranked caption pairs split 8:1:1. Exact base checkpoint, training counts, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: HumorousAI
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a state-of-the-art humor-generation competitor; implementation/version details are not reported.

model_provenance.name: LoL
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a humor-generation competitor; implementation/version details are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Phunny
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a pun-generation competitor; implementation/version details are not reported.

model_provenance.name: CLoT
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a multimodal humor-generation competitor; implementation/version details are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Detoxify
model_provenance.detail: Used for harmful-content detection; no package/model version is reported.

model_provenance.name: RoBERTa-large natural language inference model
model_provenance.detail: Used for NLI Diversity; no exact checkpoint or model source is reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 7 LLM calls per output: extractor 2, imaginator 3, generator 2
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: n_i=5 generated captions per image for pass@k
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Caption generation temperature=1; all other parameters left at defaults. Retrieval top-k jokes k=5; humor-relevant entity threshold δ=5; LLM association chain length adaptively determined with empirical average ≈4.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: All experiments conducted on 2 NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs with 16 GB; monetary/API cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Humor retrieval itself is stated not to require API calls. The paper does not explicitly sum total calls per image when five candidate captions are generated.

inference_budgets.scope: HOMER naive model in Appendix B.12
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Table 16: extractor 2, imaginator 0, generator 2 API calls
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Uses pre-processed imagination results; humor retrieval does not need API calls.
inference_budgets.detail: Included for efficiency comparison; not the main full HOMER setting.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: The pass@k metric uses n_i=5 generated captions per image, but per-baseline call budgets are not reported in the main table.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: All base LLMs use temperature=1 for caption generation; all other parameters default.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: 2 NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs are reported for all experiments; per-method compute/cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Baselines include CoT, few-shot, self-consistency, HumorousAI, LoL, Phunny, and CLoT across four base LLMs.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: HumorousAI 9 (=3 calls × 3 repeats); LoL 8 (=4 calls × 2 repeats); Phunny 9 (=3 calls × 3 repeats); CLoT 7 (=7 calls × 1 repeat); HOMER 7
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Multiple independent captions for baselines, with best one selected; exact candidate counts beyond repeats are not fully specified.
inference_budgets.detail: Used for pass@1 comparison in Table 17. The selector for the best baseline output is not reported.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: GPT-5 and other humor evaluators use temperature=0. GPT-5 dimension rankings are repeated over five trials.
inference_budgets.detail: Evaluator call counts, prompts for pass@k judging, and token limits are not reported.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Two-stage SFT followed by DPO; inference assigns a reward score to each caption.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Overall experiments use 2 NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs; fine-tuning compute details not otherwise reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Ranked caption pairs are split 8:1:1 into training, validation, and test sets, but dataset counts and training hyperparameters are not reported.

human_baselines.description: Generated captions are compared against human-written captions from Humor in AI ranking groups and Electronic Sheep High-Humor/Low-Humor groups.
human_baselines.detail: The same LLM evaluator judges generated-vs-human caption wins, but the paper does not show that human captions were produced under matched conditions, prompts, time limits, or available context.

human_baselines.description: The human-rating table compares seven generated-caption methods and does not include a human-written-caption row.
human_baselines.detail: Human raters evaluate machine-generated captions only.

human_baselines.description: Generated meme captions are evaluated against ground-truth meme captions from a public ImgFlip/MemeCap-style dataset.
human_baselines.detail: The paper does not report the human caption-generation conditions or dataset size, so matched comparability cannot be established.

human_baselines.description: LLM evaluators are tested on human-written caption pairs randomly selected across 200 contests with known rankings.
human_baselines.detail: This is a validation set for evaluator accuracy, not a generation baseline under matched human-vs-model production conditions.

mitigations: The humor retrieval database excludes cartoons/image captions and is restricted to text-only humor corpora.
The paper reports removal of duplicate jokes and jokes sharing more than 80% of English words.
The paper reports exact-match and normalized-overlap comparisons between the joke database and test captions, with zero overlap.
The test set is described as reserved for multimodal caption generation, separate from text-only humor retrieval sources.
contamination_risk.basis: The evaluated benchmarks are public New Yorker Caption Contest-derived datasets and public meme data, and later closed/proprietary LLMs such as GPT-4o, Claude-4, GPT-4.1, and GPT-5 are used for generation and/or evaluation. The paper discusses leakage only between the authors’ joke retrieval database and the test captions; it does not report decontamination or memorization checks for the base LLMs or LLM judges against the public benchmark captions/rankings. Under the audit rule for public legacy benchmarks evaluated with later proprietary models, this remains at least moderate risk.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Headline pass@k superiority of HOMER over baselines on New Yorker and meme datasets; evaluator-reliability claims; GPT-5-ranked visual understanding, humor understanding, and stylistic expression analyses; additional GPT-4.1 validation claims.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: The paper reports a human evaluation with 20 raters and 5600 rating scores, plus Pearson correlations between GPT-5 and human ratings of 0.8608 on Humor in AI and 0.8533 on Electronic Sheep. However, the human-evaluation appendix contains inconsistencies about number of raters and rating totals.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The central performance tables use GPT-5 as the primary humor evaluator. Human ratings corroborate the direction of the result but are secondary and smaller/less clearly specified than the LLM-judged pass@k evaluation.

reporting_gaps: No exact API snapshots, model versions, inference dates, or provider release identifiers are reported for GPT-4o, Claude-4, GPT-5, GPT-4.1, or Qwen-Turbo.
Exact checkpoints/access modes are not reported for Qwen-VL, LLaVA-1.5, LLaMa 3, Humor-tuned LLaMa3, RoBERTa-large NLI, or Detoxify.
Human-rater recruitment, demographics, geography, expertise, compensation, screening, and exclusion criteria are not reported.
Human-evaluation blinding, caption ordering, randomization, and whether raters saw method names are not reported.

Human-evaluation accounting is internally inconsistent: main text reports 20 raters and 5600 rating scores; Appendix B.8 procedure reports 2,800 data points from 20 images × 20 raters × 7 methods; Appendix B.8 text later says 12 human raters and gives different means from Table 13.
The paper does not clearly state how the specific captions used in human evaluation were selected from generated candidates.
The selector for the 'best' baseline output in the Appendix B.12 repeated-generation comparison is not reported.
Per-method generation budgets for CoT, few-shot, self-consistency, HumorousAI, LoL, Phunny, and CLoT in the main Table 3 are not fully reported beyond the shared pass@k setup.
Token budgets, max output lengths, top-p, frequency penalties, and other decoding parameters are not reported; only temperature and defaults are stated.
Prompts/rubrics for GPT-5/GPT-4.1 pass@k judging are not fully specified, and evaluator call counts are not reported.
The actual embedding method f_emb used for joke retrieval is not specified; the method section says it can be statistical or LM-based.
Meme dataset size, sampling procedure, and caption-selection details are not reported.
Fine-tuning hyperparameters, exact training-set sizes, and checkpoint details for Humor-tuned LLaMa3 are not reported.

The paper’s leakage analysis checks overlap between the joke database and test captions but does not test whether generation/evaluation LLMs had seen the public benchmark captions or rankings during pretraining.
Human-written caption baselines are not shown to have been produced under matched instructions, time constraints, or context relative to model outputs.
Details of Wilcoxon test units and any multiple-comparison correction are not reported.
